title: JPMorgan Kinexis Crosses $1B Daily in Tokenized Deposit Transactions
summary: JPMorgan's Kinexis platform (formerly JPM Onyx) now processes over $1 billion in daily tokenized deposit transactions for institutional clients, enabling programmable intraday liquidity, automated collateral settlement, and repo transactions on a permissioned blockchain. The platform has expanded to include cross-border USD/EUR settlement for select corporate treasury clients.
businessImpact: This is a direct competitive threat to any bank offering traditional intraday credit, repo, or collateral management products. Kinexis is effectively replacing overnight and intraday liquidity products with programmable, 24/7 tokenized alternatives — at a fraction of the operational cost. Banks that are not developing equivalent capabilities face accelerating client attrition among institutional and corporate treasury clients who manage large intraday positions.
technicalTakeaway: Permissioned blockchain settlement enables atomic delivery-vs-payment (DVP) for repo and collateral without intraday credit exposure — structurally removing friction that traditional SWIFT/Fedwire workflows cannot eliminate.
businessTakeaway: Clients using Kinexis reduce intraday credit utilization, freeing capital. Banks offering these clients traditional liquidity lines see reduced fee revenue and credit utilization as the need migrates to the platform.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasurers using Kinexis can automate collateral posting, reduce idle cash buffers, and settle cross-border transactions outside SWIFT hours — a capabilities gap that non-Kinexis bank relationships cannot currently close.

title: TCH Advances Regulated Liability Network Pilot for Tokenized Commercial Bank Money
summary: The Clearing House is piloting the Regulated Liability Network (RLN) in collaboration with major U.S. commercial banks, enabling tokenized commercial bank money to settle interbank obligations on a shared ledger without converting to central bank money. The pilot includes interbank wholesale settlement and controlled tests of tokenized deposit transfers between participating institutions.
businessImpact: The RLN represents a potential infrastructure layer that could redefine how commercial bank money moves — operating parallel to, and eventually integrated with, RTP and FedNow rails. Banks that participate early establish influence over how the network rules and fee structures are set. Banks that wait risk being price-takers on a critical infrastructure they don't control. Treasury product teams need to assess whether RLN participation is a strategic priority before governance structures solidify.
technicalTakeaway: RLN uses a shared ledger where each participating bank's tokenized deposits represent claims on the issuing institution — preserving the existing two-tier banking structure while enabling programmable settlement.
businessTakeaway: Early participation shapes network rules, fee economics, and interoperability standards. Positioning now has long-term strategic value disproportionate to the current pilot scale.
treasuryTakeaway: If RLN scales, corporate treasury clients will eventually be able to move tokenized deposits across banks in real time, fundamentally altering how banks compete for operating account balances.

title: MoneyGram Expands Stablecoin-Settled Cross-Border B2B Payments Across 20 Markets
summary: MoneyGram has extended its stablecoin settlement infrastructure — built on USDC and the Stellar network — to B2B cross-border payment corridors in 20 markets, enabling businesses to send and receive international payments that settle in under 3 minutes at fees substantially below correspondent banking rates. The service targets SME importers/exporters and mid-market treasury operations.
businessImpact: MoneyGram is using stablecoin rails to commoditize cross-border correspondent banking fees — one of the highest-margin services in corporate treasury banking. Banks with significant international trade finance and FX payment revenues from mid-market corporate clients face direct displacement risk, particularly in SME and mid-market segments where relationships are less entrenched than in large corporate banking.
technicalTakeaway: Stellar network + USDC settlement enables final settlement in 3-5 seconds, removing the 1-3 day correspondent banking float and enabling 24/7 operation across time zones — structural advantages traditional SWIFT rails cannot replicate without multi-party coordination.
businessTakeaway: Mid-market corporate clients paying 100-300bps for cross-border wires are an accessible target for MoneyGram's pricing. Banks need a clear competitive response or risk pricing pressure on cross-border FX fees.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasurers managing international payables/receivables in mid-market companies now have a credible, regulated alternative to correspondent banking for routine cross-border transactions.

title: Western Union Launches Digital B2B Platform Targeting Corporate Treasury Payments
summary: Western Union has launched a dedicated B2B digital payments platform integrating multiple settlement rails including stablecoin-adjacent digital wallets, targeting the corporate treasury segment with real-time FX locking, multi-currency accounts, and API-first integration for ERP/TMS systems. The platform directly targets mid-market corporate clients managing cross-border supplier and vendor payments.
businessImpact: Western Union's B2B pivot represents a significant escalation from its B2C remittance roots. By combining its existing cross-border infrastructure with API-first treasury integrations and competitive FX pricing, it directly targets the mid-market corporate segment — a client base that bank treasury services have historically served with minimum differentiation. Banks need to evaluate whether their B2B payment product and pricing is defensible against a well-capitalized, global-brand competitor with no legacy banking cost structure.
technicalTakeaway: API-first ERP/TMS integration lowers the switching cost for corporate clients — once connected at the system level, switching back to bank wires requires a full TMS re-integration project.
businessTakeaway: The API-first, ERP-integrated approach creates sticky relationship dynamics. Banks that do not offer equivalent TMS/ERP integrations are at a structural disadvantage in retaining mid-market corporate clients.
treasuryTakeaway: Mid-market treasurers gain access to real-time FX rate locking and same-day cross-border settlement via a single API — capabilities traditionally available only to large-cap clients at major banks.

title: Federal Reserve Issues Supervisory Guidance on Bank Engagement with Stablecoins and Tokenized Deposits
summary: The Federal Reserve issued updated supervisory guidance clarifying permissible bank activities related to stablecoins and tokenized deposits, drawing a regulatory distinction between 'tokenized deposits' (which remain subject to standard deposit insurance and reserve requirements) and 'payment stablecoins' (which may be issued by non-bank entities under proposed GENIUS Act framework). Banks may issue tokenized deposits under existing charters with enhanced liquidity reporting.
businessImpact: This guidance provides the clearest regulatory green light to date for bank-issued tokenized deposits, while maintaining a competitive moat against non-bank stablecoin issuers — who face a more burdensome separate licensing regime. Banks with strong compliance infrastructure should treat this as a window to move aggressively on tokenized deposit products before non-bank stablecoin issuers gain equivalent access. The longer the window, the larger the first-mover advantage.
technicalTakeaway: Tokenized deposits under this framework must maintain 1:1 backing in central bank reserves or liquid assets, with enhanced real-time liquidity reporting — higher ops cost than non-bank stablecoin models but significantly lower than feared.
businessTakeaway: Banks gain a regulatory-endorsed competitive moat: they can offer tokenized deposits under existing charter authority, while non-bank competitors must await new licensing rules. This is a time-limited strategic window.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate clients seeking programmable treasury functionality gain access to bank-issued tokenized deposits with FDIC insurance clarity — removing the primary adoption barrier for large corporate treasuries.

title: FedNow Surpasses 1,000 Participating Financial Institutions
summary: The FedNow Service has crossed 1,000 participating financial institutions, with transaction volume growing 340% year-over-year. However, send-side activation remains the critical constraint — the majority of participating institutions are receive-only, limiting the network's utility for corporate clients requiring bidirectional real-time payment capability.
businessImpact: The send-side activation gap is the single most actionable near-term opportunity in the FedNow market. Banks that fully activate send capability — and market it clearly to corporate treasury clients — gain a differentiated competitive position over the majority of the FedNow network. For treasury product teams, the immediate priority is identifying which corporate client segments have the highest demand for real-time outbound payments (payroll, insurance disbursements, B2B vendor payments) and activating send capability for those use cases first.
technicalTakeaway: The receive-only imbalance means that even a bank with 1,000 FedNow connections cannot guarantee real-time payment delivery to all counterparties — limiting the product pitch to corporate clients with known counterparty coverage.
businessTakeaway: First-mover advantage on full send/receive activation with clear corporate client marketing is a near-term, high-ROI product move that most competitors have not yet executed.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasury clients evaluating FedNow enablement need counterparty coverage maps before committing workflows — banks that provide this proactively will close treasury relationships faster.

title: RTP Network Sets Transaction Volume Record; B2B Use Cases Now Dominant
summary: The RTP network set a new monthly transaction volume record, with B2B use cases now representing over 60% of transaction value — up from 35% two years ago. Dominant use cases include trade payables acceleration, just-in-time vendor payments, and insurance claims disbursement. The $1M per-transaction limit continues to be the primary cited barrier for large corporate adoption.
businessImpact: The B2B dominance shift validates real-time payments as a genuine treasury management tool, not just a consumer payment rail. Banks have a clear product narrative to bring to corporate treasury clients: RTP is now primary infrastructure for B2B workflows, not a novelty. The $1M limit barrier is a known ceiling — banks should be proactive in helping corporate clients structure transactions within limits and advocating for limit increases with TCH.
technicalTakeaway: The $1M transaction limit effectively excludes high-value treasury flows like large intercompany transfers, M&A escrow disbursements, and large supplier payments — use cases that could dramatically increase RTP volume if the limit were raised.
businessTakeaway: Banks should be building case studies and client ROI models around B2B RTP use cases now — the market has validated them, but most corporate treasury clients still have not been formally presented with a use-case-specific business case by their banking partners.
treasuryTakeaway: Treasury teams running B2B payment operations should evaluate which vendor payment workflows can be shifted to RTP for same-day cash application, eliminating remittance reconciliation delays associated with ACH.

title: RTP and FedNow: Where's the Hockey Stick?
summary: Digital Transactions analysis examines why RTP and FedNow volume growth has been linear rather than exponential, identifying three structural barriers: (1) lack of corporate ERP integration by banks, (2) absence of Request for Payment (RfP) workflows in mainstream corporate treasury systems, and (3) pricing models that do not create clear ROI narratives for corporate clients to switch from ACH.
businessImpact: This is a direct product strategy signal. The hockey stick in real-time payments volume will not come from consumer adoption — it will come from banks solving the three barriers identified here for corporate clients. Banks that invest in ERP integration, RfP workflow enablement, and clear ACH-displacement ROI models will capture disproportionate share of the coming volume inflection. The analysis identifies a market gap that is solvable through product investment, not a fundamental demand problem.
technicalTakeaway: ERP integration is the critical missing link — without native RTP payment initiation in Oracle, SAP, and Microsoft Dynamics treasury modules, corporate AP teams cannot switch from ACH batch without manual workflow changes they won't make.
businessTakeaway: Banks that partner with or build ERP connectors for RTP/FedNow payment initiation have a direct product moat. This is not a technology problem — it's a distribution and integration problem.
treasuryTakeaway: Treasury teams managing AP workflows in major ERPs should pressure banking partners to deliver certified RTP/FedNow payment modules — the banks that deliver this first will win operating account relationships.

title: Same-Day ACH Sets Volume Record — Signals Continued ACH Relevance in Multi-Rail Strategy
summary: NACHA reported Same-Day ACH volume of 900 million transactions in Q1 2025, a 31% year-over-year increase, with the majority of volume from B2B payroll, vendor payments, and government disbursements. NACHA indicated a roadmap to extend Same-Day ACH processing windows and expand the per-transaction limit to $1M to better compete with RTP and FedNow use cases.
businessImpact: The ACH limit increase to $1M would directly overlap with the primary use-case gap cited as a barrier to RTP corporate adoption — creating a multi-rail market where RTP, FedNow, and enhanced Same-Day ACH compete for the same B2B corporate wallet. Banks with multi-rail orchestration capabilities can serve this as a feature; banks with siloed ACH and RTP products will face margin compression as clients route transactions to the cheapest available rail. A clear multi-rail pricing and orchestration strategy is now a product necessity.
technicalTakeaway: An ACH limit increase to $1M effectively makes Same-Day ACH a direct substitute for most RTP use cases at lower per-transaction fees — narrowing the product differentiation argument for real-time rails to speed and 24/7 availability only.
businessTakeaway: Banks need a multi-rail strategy that positions each rail by use case ROI, not just availability — otherwise clients self-select to cheapest rail and bank pricing power erodes.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasury teams should revisit payment rail selection criteria annually as limits and pricing evolve — a multi-rail strategy requires active governance, not a one-time implementation.

title: Fiserv Launches 'Now' Platform — Unified RTP/FedNow Orchestration for Bank Clients
summary: Fiserv launched its 'Now' instant payments platform, providing a single API for bank clients to orchestrate payments across RTP and FedNow rails with intelligent routing, fraud scoring, and real-time confirmation. The platform includes pre-built integrations for major core banking systems and is positioned to accelerate mid-tier bank real-time payment activation.
businessImpact: Fiserv's unified platform significantly reduces the technical barrier for mid-tier and community banks to activate RTP and FedNow simultaneously — meaning the supply side of the network is about to expand rapidly. For larger banks, this signals that the competitive advantage of simply being on real-time rails is eroding; the differentiation will shift entirely to use-case depth, corporate client integration quality, and fraud controls. Larger banks should accelerate their product differentiation strategies before the network reaches critical mass of participants.
technicalTakeaway: Unified API with intelligent routing enables real-time rail selection based on counterparty capability, transaction size, and cost — a capability most bank-built solutions do not yet have.
businessTakeaway: As Fiserv enables hundreds of additional banks, the 'we're on real-time rails' pitch loses differentiation value. Banks must shift product narratives to client-facing capabilities, not infrastructure availability.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate clients gain broader counterparty coverage as more banks activate, reducing the coverage gap that has been a friction point for enterprise adoption.

title: CFPB Proposes Expanded Fraud Liability for Banks on Authorized Push Payment Scams
summary: The CFPB proposed extending bank liability to Authorized Push Payment (APP) fraud on real-time rails — where consumers are tricked into authorizing fraudulent transfers. The proposal would require banks to reimburse victims within 5 business days and implement enhanced pre-authorization screening, mirroring UK Payment Systems Regulator mandatory reimbursement rules implemented in 2024.
businessImpact: If enacted, this regulation would materially increase the operational cost of real-time payment products — adding fraud reimbursement liability that does not exist for ACH or card payments under current frameworks. Banks need to immediately assess their fraud detection capabilities on real-time rails and model the potential liability exposure. Banks that treat real-time payment fraud controls as a cost center rather than a strategic investment will face regulatory risk and client attrition if fraud rates exceed peer benchmarks.
technicalTakeaway: Pre-authorization screening requirements will require real-time payee verification — driving adoption of account verification services and confirmation of payee (CoP) infrastructure, which most U.S. banks have not yet deployed.
businessTakeaway: Banks with strong fraud analytics capabilities gain a competitive advantage: they can offer real-time payment products with lower fraud-related pricing risk. Banks with weak controls face regulatory penalty exposure and potential product suspension.
treasuryTakeaway: Corporate treasury teams should understand how their bank's fraud controls affect real-time payment SLAs and whether fraud-related holds or reversals could disrupt time-sensitive payment workflows.

title: McKinsey Global Payments 2025: Real-Time Rails Will Capture 30% of Global B2B Payment Value by 2028
summary: McKinsey's annual global payments report projects real-time payment rails will carry 30% of global B2B payment value by 2028, up from 8% in 2024, driven primarily by regulatory mandates in Europe and Asia, corporate ERP integration maturity, and declining per-transaction costs. U.S. market is projected to lag European adoption by 18-24 months without regulatory catalysts.
businessImpact: The 18-24 month U.S. lag relative to global peers is both a risk and an opportunity. Banks with global corporate clients already operating on real-time rails in Europe and Asia will face client pressure to deliver equivalent capability in the U.S. — creating a defined client mandate rather than a speculative product bet. For U.S. banks, aligning real-time payment product roadmaps to the timelines of their most globally active corporate clients is the highest-conviction go-to-market strategy.
technicalTakeaway: Global B2B real-time payment growth is driven by ISO 20022 standardization enabling structured data rich payments — eliminating remittance reconciliation, the #1 friction point for corporate AP/AR teams adopting real-time rails.
businessTakeaway: The ROI case for corporate real-time payment adoption hinges on structured data — banks that deliver ISO 20022 rich data payments close deals faster than those selling speed alone.
treasuryTakeaway: Treasury teams at multinationals should benchmark U.S. payment capabilities against their European operations — the gap often becomes a board-level treasury transformation priority once quantified.
